coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic performance of TiO2/g-C3N4 nanocomposite modified with carbon quantum dots for effective dye degradation: Experiments and DFT studies

coredata.dc:description: The water resources have been contaminated by the synthetic dyes that requires the effective wastewater treatment solutions due to the significant environmental and health challenges. This study reports the synthesis of a CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 ternary composite using a hydrothermal route to improve the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB). The structural and morphological properties of the synthesized composite are investigated using FTIR, XRD, Raman, SEM, TEM, and UV-Vis spectroscopy. The photocatalytic activity evaluated under UV light, demonstrates about 98.9 % MB degradation efficiency under optimized conditions of pH, catalyst dosage, and dye concentration. The findings emphasize the synergistic effects of the composite resulting with the enhanced photocatalytic performance. The First-Principles Density function theory (DFT) based calculations are carried out to elucidate the mechanism for improving the performance of the composite system. This work presents a facile method for treating wastewater and highlighted the potential of CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposites for environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic performance of TiO2/g-C3N4 nanocomposite modified with carbon quantum dots for effective dye degradation: Experiments and DFT studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The water resources have been contaminated by the synthetic dyes that requires the effective wastewater treatment solutions due to the significant environmental and health challenges. This study reports the synthesis of a CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 ternary composite using a hydrothermal route to improve the photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB). The structural and morphological properties of the synthesized composite are investigated using FTIR, XRD, Raman, SEM, TEM, and UV-Vis spectroscopy. The photocatalytic activity evaluated under UV light, demonstrates about 98.9 % MB degradation efficiency under optimized conditions of pH, catalyst dosage, and dye concentration. The findings emphasize the synergistic effects of the composite resulting with the enhanced photocatalytic performance. The First-Principles Density function theory (DFT) based calculations are carried out to elucidate the mechanism for improving the performance of the composite system. This work presents a facile method for treating wastewater and highlighted the potential of CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposites for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: Rivers, lakes, and fresh groundwater constitute 70 % of the earth's surface, while the potable water is only 0.03 % [1–4]. This scarcity has exacerbated the unavailability of clean water for approximately 750 million people worldwide. Furthermore, the presence of micro-pollutants in water bodies may lead to the significant environmental and health risks [1]. In the twenty-first century, the consequences of environmental pollution due to both natural and human activities have become a major global concern, affecting human and aquatic life [1]. The textile industry has been focused because of the release of the toxic and carcinogenic dyes which are harmful to all tropical species [5]. Furthermore, the dyes that incorporate waste materials from various industries, such as food production, leather processing, paper manufacturing, printing and the use of paints and cosmetics, holds high environmental risks as they can leak into freshwater sources [6]. Azo dyes are the most prevalent one among dye effluents and pose major risks. Their complexity lies in the aromatic molecular structure, which is generally stable in nature [7]. Among advanced oxidation process, photocatalysis has been emerged as a sustainable and non-toxic route for dye degradation without generating harmful secondary pollutants [8]. MB dye is degraded into harmless byproducts including water,CO2 and inorganic ions like ammonium, nitrate and sulfate [9]. Two-dimensional materials are composed of numerous thin layers, each with a thickness of few nanometers [10–12]. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a metal-free semiconductor with distinct physicochemical and photocatalytic properties. The electrons produced via photoexcitation in g-C3N4 have shown high thermodynamic potential to promote the reduction of several organic molecules into H2O and CO2. Nonetheless, bulk g-C3N4 has exhibited multiple deficiencies, including a limited specific surface area and an increased recombination rate [13].

The green synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using natural and renewable resources as precursors is an environmentally sustainable method, for reducing the reliance on toxic chemicals. Furthermore, these environmentally sustainable processes helps in waste reduction through the use of cost effective materials. The CQDs with different chemicals, can be synthesized by using arc-discharge, laser ablation, hydrothermal methods, plasma, ultrasonication, microwave processes, chemical exfoliation, pyrolysis, precipitation, among others [14]. Moreover, the fruit waste may lead to breeding of dangerous insects and flies like mosquitoes, cockroaches etc. The CQDs have sp2 or sp3 carbon containing oxygen. CQDs contains many functional group like carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, epoxy and aldehyde groups that improve the solubility and polymerization ability of CQDs [15]. Basha et al.,[16] prepared CDs from teak leaves biomass by ultrasonication (at 40 kHz) utilizing leaves as the biomass source. They layered with graphene oxide using one step ultrasonication method and performed adsorption studies for MB dye which shows the significant removal rate of 50.1 %, with an impressive adsorption capacity of 735.2 mg g−1, and regeneration study of approximately 77.1 % of the recovered MB dye. CDs has better interaction with the organic dye MB as it contains various functional group that forms electrostatic attraction and hydrogen bond towards the dye, it also acts as a pore filling to enhance the adsorption efficiency of graphene oxide. Shahraki et al., [17] prepared carbon dots (CDs) from papaya peel using hydrothermal method. The synthesized RGO/CDs nanocomposites have demonstrated significant photocatalytic activity, achieving 87 % degradation of MB dye in 135 min with a 2:1 mass ratio. CDs increased the surface area and pore volume, facilitating better ion penetration, which is essential for photocatalytic applications.

Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is extensively used in photocatalysis due to its chemical stability, affordability and non-toxicity. However, the limited surface area and high recombination rate of electron-hole pairs continue to impede its implementation [18]. Consequently, several efforts have been taken to enhance charge separation by modifying the surface or bulk characteristics of TiO2, doping, metal deposition, size reduction, and semiconductor coupling, which also improves the photocatalytic activity. The photo-response of TiO2 can be enhanced by integrating it with a semiconductor which exhibits a lower bandgap and a superior conduction band [19].

Wang et al., [20] developed a ternary catalytic system, g-C3N4/NCDs/Ag, to breakdown the organic contaminants in aqueous solutions. This system relies on an interfacial electronic control mechanism, by the dual incorporation of NCDs and Ag NPs. The NCDs has the potential to enhance the charge carrier mobility by bridging electron pathways. Notably, the g-C3N4/NCDs/Ag catalyst has showed a substantial increase in catalytic activity, with degradation rates for MO and Rh B surpassing those of pure g-C3N4 by factors of 13.5 and 12.9, respectively. Cui et al. [21] synthesized C-doped TiO2 coupled with g-C3N4 photocatalysts through a combined hydrothermal and high-temperature calcination process. The results revealed that the carbon doping has induced a shift in the binding energy of TiO2 towards lower values, as an indicative in the modification of its surface electronic structure and a reduction in its valence state. Notably, the resulting C-TiO2/g-C3N4 composite has achieved the degradation efficiencies of 92.6 % for methylene blue (MB) and 95.3 % for tetracycline (TC). Chen et al. [22] synthesized a g-C3N4/NGQDs/TiO2 composite using a calcination method. This composite have exhibited a narrower bandgap of 2.24 eV, which is lower than that of pure TiO2 and g-C3N4, enabling improved visible-light absorption. As a result, the composite has showed an enhanced photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin (CIP), reaching 80 % degradation within 100 min under visible light irradiation. Liu et al.[23] fabricated a three-dimensional ordered macroporous carbon quantum dots/TiO2/g-C3N4 composite (denoted as 1 % CQDs/3DTCN) to enhance its photocatalytic activity. This enhancement was evaluated by degrading RhB dye under simulated sunlight irradiation. The 1 % CQDs/3DTCN composite has shown significantly higher degradation efficiency (98 %) compared to pristine 3DTCN (42 %) after 80 min of irradiation.

This study explores the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from papaya peel waste, a sustainable source using hydrothermal method. The hydrothermal method employed in this study has several advantages, including the potential to obtain CQDs with high quantum yield and a disordered structure, which can further improve the photocatalytic performance. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the utilization of CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposites for the photocatalytic degradation of MB pollutants under UV irradiation and Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations to analyse the mechanism. This work demonstrates the development of an efficient and sustainable photocatalyst by evaluating its performance, such as catalyst dosage, dye concentration, and solution pH effects. Furthermore, First-principles Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were performed to elucidate a plausible mechanism to enhance the performance of the composite system.

Melamine (with 98 % purity) and TiO2 (98 % purity) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Distilled water was used throughout the experiments. Papaya was collected from a local fruit stall in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia.

To synthesize g-C3N4, 5 g of melamine was placed in a crucible and calcined at 500 °C for 3 h under the ambient air atmosphere. A heating rate of 20 °C/min was maintained throughout the calcination process and obtained a light-yellow g-C3N4 powder. This method was adapted from a previously reported procedure [21].

CQDs were prepared from papaya peel waste. Initially, the peels were chopped and dried overnight in an oven at 60 °C. Subsequently, 8 g of dried peel powder was mixed with 100 mL of distilled water (DW) and transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Hydrothermal treatment was carried out at 200 °C for 24 h. After the cooling phase, the resulting dark brown solution was centrifuged to isolate the CQD solution. The synthesized CQDs were stored at 4 °C until subsequent use [24].

To synthesize CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposites, a two-step process was employed. In the process, the g-C3N4/TiO2 composite was prepared via a hydrothermal method. 276 mg of g-C3N4 and 120 mg of TiO2 were made into a homogeneous mixture and dispersed in 120 mL of DW through ultrasonication. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor and heated at 140 °C for 12 h. The resulting product was washed with DW and ethanol to remove impurities, further dried the washed sample at 60 °C for 7 h in an oven, and stored for future use [25]. Subsequently, 0.1 g of the g-C3N4/TiO2 composite was dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water through sonication. 1 mL of CQDs was added to this dispersion and stirred for 24 h. The final CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposite was obtained after washing with DW and ethanol and dried for 7 h at 60 °C. The formation process of CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 are described in Figure S1 .

To assess the photocatalytic activity of the synthesized materials, a photocatalytic degradation experiment was conducted using a 95 W UV lamp as the light source. A 0.1 g sample of the catalyst was added to 60 mL of a 10 ppm MB solution. The mixture was stirred in the dark for 30 min to attain adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then, the UV lamp was turned on to initiate the photocatalytic reaction. At 15 min intervals, 5 mL aliquots of the mixture were withdrawn, centrifuged to remove the catalyst, and analyzed using UV-Vis spectrophotometry. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency was calculated using the formula (1)
(1) Degradation % = C0 – Ct / C0 * 100
where C0 and Ct represent the MB concentrations in ppm initially and at time t, respectively.

All the first-principles density function theory (DFT) based calculations were performed using QuantumWise ATK software, [25,26]. The slab model of the heterojunction formed on the surface of TiO2 (110), g-C3N4 (001) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (001) is shown in (
Fig. 1 (a-d)). The structures were aligned to minimize the lattice mismatch (5.6 %) by employing a 2 × 2 supercell for TiO2 (110) with four layers. A 6-layer vacuum region (approximately 20 Å) was introduced around the structure to prevent self-interactions arising from the periodic boundary conditions. All the calculations were performed using the Linear Combination of Atomic Orbitals (LCAO) method, with the exchange-correlation effects described by the PBE-GGA functional [27] along with the semi-empirical Grimme DFT-D3 [28] for the 94 Elements H-Pu [28] van der Waal’s corrections to map the van der Waal’s interactions. The k-point mesh was determined from the Monkhorst-Pack grid for TiO2 (110) and heterojunction as 3 × 4 × 1 and for g-C3N4 monolayer as 3 × 3 × 1 shifted to the gamma-point of the Brillouin Zone. The energy cut-off was set to 3400 eV. The electronic optimization and geometric relaxation were performed until the energy and forces converged to 10−4 eV and 0.05 eV/Å, respectively.

The XRD patterns have revealed the presence of characteristic peaks corresponding to g-C3N4 and TiO2 in the composite, as shown in Fig. 2. The peaks at 13.1° and 27.25° are attributed to the (100) and (002) planes of g-C3N4, and confirms the formation of a hexagonal graphite-like structure (JCPDS: 87–1526) [29]. The peaks at 25.3°, 38.1°, 48.2°, 54.2°, and 62.5° correspond to the (101), (004), (200), (211), and (204) planes of anatase TiO2, respectively (JCPDS #75–1537) [30]. The observed shift of the g-C3N4 diffraction peak to a lower 2θ angle following TiO2 coupling has indicated a close association between g-C3N4 and TiO2 [31]. The high positive charge of Ti4+ ions and highly electron-rich graphitic carbon nitride could be the reason for excellent spreading of g-C₃N₄ over the titanium oxide surface. No distinct diffraction peaks were observed for CQDs, likely due to their low concentration and high dispersion on the surface of the g-C3N4/TiO2 composite with the pure crystallinity of the composite [23]. The notably small particle size of the CQDs, coupled with their uniform dispersion is a potential factor influencing the observed outcome [32]. The structural parameters, including crystalline size, dislocation density, and microstrain, for the CQDs/g-C₃N₄/TiO₂ nanocomposite are listed in Table S1.

Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectrum of CQDs, g-C3N4, TiO2, g-C3N4/TiO2, and CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposites. For g-C3N4, the peak at 814 cm⁻¹ represents the characteristic of the tri-s-triazine ring, while the broad band over 3000–3500 cm⁻¹ corresponds to N-H and O-H stretching vibrations [33–35] The peaks in the 1200–1650 cm⁻¹ region are associated with CN and C-N stretching vibrations. TiO2 exhibits a characteristic peak at 447 cm⁻¹ confirming the anatase phase [36], and a broad peak at 1628 cm⁻¹ due to adsorption of water molecules. The g-C3N4-TiO2 composite shows similar peaks, indicating the presence of both TiO2 and adsorbed water. The FTIR spectrum of CQDs exhibits a broad peak between 3100 and 3440 cm⁻¹ attributed to O-H and N-H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1439 cm⁻¹ , 1619 cm⁻¹ , and 1398 cm⁻¹ are assigned to CC, CO, and C-H bonds, respectively. The lower wavenumber region shows an additional C-O and C-H vibrations [37]. The overlapping peaks in the 1200–1650 cm⁻¹ region has indicated the interactions between the functional groups of g-C3N4, TiO2, and CQDs. The broad O-H absorption band confirms the presence of adsorbed water and hydroxyl groups from CQDs and g-C3N4 which made a good hydrophilicity for composites and effectively enhanced its photocatalysis [36,38].

The Raman spectra have provided further evidence for the composite formation, as shown in Fig. 4. TiO2, in its anatase phase, exhibits characteristic Raman peaks at 144, 203, 375, 507, and 640 cm⁻¹ . The vibrations at 753, 977, 1120, 1156, 1236 and 1314 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of aromatic C-N heterocycle characteristic to melem [39]. The peaks at 700–1000 cm−1 has indicated the different types of ring breathing modes of s-triazine [39]. The peak located at ∼1600 cm−1 has been ascribed to the G band, which arises due to stretching vibrations of CN (E2 g symmetry) of any pair of sp2 sites in aromatic rings. The D peak was observed near 1350 cm−1 and this band arises from the breathing mode (A1 g symmetry) vibration modes of the CN heterocycle in g-C3N4 of sp2 sites in clusters of six-fold aromatic rings, which confirms the graphite like structure [40] . TiO2, in its anatase phase, exhibits characteristic Raman peaks at 140 (Eg), 388 (B1 g), 510 (A1 g), and 641(Eg) cm⁻¹ , an intense peak at 140 cm⁻¹ which is attributed to the symmetric O-Ti-O vibration peak. This shows that there is no change in the phase of the TiO2 during the composite formation [41].

Fig. 5 shows TGA curve for CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposite for heating the temperature from 0 to 900 °C in an air atmosphere at a rate of 10 °C/min. The composite has shown two major weight loss, the first weight loss with 3.61 % is a quite insignificant one, usually at this region the weight loss may be due to the removal of moisture from the surface of the composite and release of volatile organic compounds [42,43]. As the temperature increases from 443 °C, the gases containing oxygen moieties are evolved from the surface of CQDs but at a lesser extent this could be due to the lower concentration of CQDs [44]. A significant weight loss of 50.19 % was observed between 443 and 640 °C. This second major decomposition indicates that g-C3N4 was influenced by the earlier decomposition. Further, it is consistent with observations in Khan et al. [45], which shows that g-C3N4 decomposition begins at 500 °C. It is noticed that the decomposition becomes profoundly steeper attributing to the structural changes of g-C3N4 possibly due to the loss of tri-s-triazine-based units [45]. The final decomposition product is approximately 46.2 % of the original mass, remaining as a solid residue. Therefore, g-C3N4 has maintained the thermal stability up to 640 °C before the complete decomposition. The residue, likely containing TiO2 has considerably enhanced the composite's thermal stability and observed to be consistent with the findings reported by Madima et al. [31].

Fig. 6 depicts the UV-Vis absorption spectra of the synthesized materials. Pure g-C3N4 exhibits absorption peaks at 241 nm and 326 nm [46], that are corresponding to π → π* transitions due to lone pair of N within the carbon and nitrogen core [47]. A shoulder peak at 388 nm is due to graphite-like structure existing in the prepared samples. TiO2 was observed with an absorption peak at 323 nm, indicating the significant UV absorption [48]. The CQDs absorption bands were observed at 317 and 250 nm, associated with n-π* and π-π* transitions of surface functional groups and CC bonds, respectively [49]. After the incorporation of CQDs into the g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposite the absorption spectrum is extended into the visible region, caused due to the synergistic effect of the material [50,51]. In Figure S2 the band gap energy calculations made for the synthesized materials are provided [52–54]. Pure g-C3N4 and TiO2 are characterized by the band gaps of 2.63 and 2.8 eV, respectively. The CQDs possess a higher band gap of 4.4 eV, due to quantum confinement effect, which indicates their stability under light exposure. The incorporation of CQDs into the g-C3N4 /TiO2 composite results in a reduced band gap of 1.6 eV, and further the photocatalytic activity was enhanced due to the charge separation and light absorption.

The surface morphology of the synthesized CQDs/g-C₃N₄/TiO₂ nanocomposites were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) at different magnifications. The SEM images shown in ( Fig. 7 (a-d)) indicates that the CQDs/g-C₃N₄/TiO₂ nanocomposites have comprised of regular morphology. The g-C₃N₄ was observed with the flaky and lamellar structure like morphology. Also, it can be clearly observed that spherical TiO2 particles are scattered on the g-C3N4 surface. These CQDs are observed to be evenly distributed with the small size distribution on the surface of g-C3N4. Fig. 7 c shows the rough surface of the material, that enhances the photocatalysis process by increasing the surface area available for light absorption and reaction.

The CQDs/g-C₃N₄/TiO₂ nanocomposite was examined using TEM with different magnifications. From TEM images, it can be inferred that the g-C3N4 shows a sheet like structure. Fig. 8 (a-c) shows the dark spots indicating the presence of carbon quantum dot. TiO2 have a spherical morphology, as clearly depicted in Fig. 8 (c). The histogram of CQDS size distribution was plotted in Figure S3 and observed the diameter around 4.76 nm. The findings indicated the formation of nanocomposite where the CQDs and TiO2 are well incorporated on the surface of g-C3N4 sheets. From the SAED pattern shown in Fig. 8 (d), it can be confirmed that the synthesized composite is polycrystalline.

UV-Vis spectroscopy was utilized to monitor the degradation of MB dye by observing the reduction in its characteristic absorption peak at 664 nm. Fig. 9 shows the absorbance spectra of CQDs/g-C₃N₄/TiO₂ recorded at different time intervals (0–120 min) and it clearly shows that there is a decrease in absorbance with increasing time. Notably, the MB dye was completely degraded for 120 min. The comparison of MB dye degradation with g-C3N4, TiO2 and CQDs/g-C₃N₄/TiO₂ nanocomposites under the UV light with 120 min are shown in Figure S4. g-C3N4 showed 64 % of degradation efficiency against MB where as TiO2 demonstrated a higher degradation efficiency of 75 % under the same condition as followed. This difference in the efficiency could be due to the fact that TiO2 has a wider band gap, active under UV light allowing it to absorb the light more efficiently. In contrast, the band gap for g-C3N4 is narrower making it comparatively less active under UV light than TiO2. Although the energy required for the activation of g-C3N4 is lower to generate electron-hole pair, its limited activity has negatively influenced the degradation efficiency of MB. However, g-C3N4 and TiO2 are combined with CQDs to form CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 composite, the degradation efficiency has increased considerably to 98.9 % within in 120 min. This increase could be related to the role of CQDs as an electron reservoir in the system. The reduction in the recombination rate and increasing the availability of free electron for redox reaction are important for the degradation of organic pollutants [54]. Thus, CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 composite has exhibited a greater degradation efficiency more than that of g-C3N4 and TiO2 and highlighted the synergistic effects of composite for enhancing the degradation efficiency.

The dye industries are discharging wastewater with different pH values into the environment. The pH level plays a vital role in the photocatalytic degradation process of different pollutants present in wastewater. This has a substantial impact on interfacial processes, influencing both anodic and cathodic chemical reactions by modifying the charge produced on the catalyst surface. The pH level of the solution has substantially influenced the photocatalytic degradation of MB [52,53]. The degradation activity of CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposites with MB dye was tested at pH levels of 2, 4, 7, 8 and 9, using 1 N HCL and 1 N NaOH in order to obtain the desired pH following the same methodology for the photocatalytic experiment. The degradation percentages recorded are 98.8, 91.44, 83.97, 89.21 and 64.37 %, respectively (
Fig. 10 (a)). At pH 2, acidic conditions favor the degradation process due to enhanced adsorption of the cationic dye onto the negatively charged surface of the photocatalyst [55]. As the pH level was increased, the surface charge of the photocatalyst becomes less negative, by reducing the electrostatic attraction between the catalyst and the dye molecules [56]. Additionally, at higher pH values, the hydroxyl radicals, which are crucial for the degradation process have decreased the degradation efficiency. Therefore, acidic conditions are optimal for the photocatalytic degradation of MB using the synthesized materials [19,54]. The negative charge of the dye has increased its solubility in acidic environments, facilitating its adsorption onto the catalyst. Furthermore, at lower pH levels, the electrostatic attraction between the dye anions and the positively charged catalyst surface enhances degradation, whereas higher pH levels have resulted in electrostatic repulsion for decreasing efficiency [57]. Also, pH has influenced the generation of reactive species, which is essential for the photocatalytic process [58]. Therefore, in this study, acidic conditions are optimal for the photocatalytic degradation of MB using the synthesized materials. The efficiency of photocatalytic degradation is determined by the C/C0 ratio, where C0 represents the initial concentration and C denotes the residual concentration of the MB solution, as displayed in Fig. 10 (b). Photocatalytic degradation of the CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposite was significantly higher at pH 2 for 120 min of UV irradiation compared to the results obtained at pH 4, 7, 8, and 9. The rate constant for the catalytic decomposition of MB with different pH was analysed by a first-order kinetic model. Fig. 10 (c) shows the kinetic studies for the prepared composites of different pH values of 2, 4, 7,8 and 9.

The photocatalytic efficiency of the composites for MB degradation was investigated by varying the catalyst dosage, as presented in Fig. 11 . The different catalyst amounts (0.05, 0.075, 0.1 and 0.15 g) were tested with a 10 ppm MB dye solution at pH 2 under UV light irradiation for 120 min. As shown in Fig. 11 (a), increasing the catalyst dosage to 0.05, 0.075 and 0.1 has led to a corresponding increase in MB degradation efficiency as 44, 49 and 98.9 %, respectively. This is related to the higher number of active sites available for photocatalytic reactions at high catalyst amount. This enhancement in degradation efficiency with the increasing amount of photocatalysts was attributed to the larger production of radicals. However, excessive catalyst dosage can lead to agglomeration and precipitation, resulting in larger particle sizes and reduced specific surface area at 0.15 g and the efficiency was dropped to 64 % [59]. This can hinder the adsorption of dye molecules and limit the number of active sites, which decreases the overall degradation efficiency [60]. This reduction in degradation efficiency with increased catalyst dosage may be linked to turbidity [60]. Additionally, the efficiency of active sites generating free radicals were reduced due to a reduction in light absorption [61]. Fig. 11 (b) demonstrates that the CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposite, at a catalyst dosage of 0.1 g in 60 mL MB solution (pH 2, 120 min UV irradiation), exhibits higher photocatalytic degradation than other catalyst dosage tested. The rate constant for the catalytic decomposition of MB with different dosage was assessed by using a first-order kinetic model. Fig. 11 (c) displays the first order kinetic studies for the CQDs/g-C₃N₄/TiO₂ nanocomposites of different dosage (0.05, 0.075, 0.1 and 0.15 g).

The pollutant concentration can impact the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. To investigate this, MB degradation was evaluated at its different concentrations (10, 20, 40 and 60 ppm). As the MB concentration was increased, the degradation efficiency got decreased from 98 to 65, 1.2 and finally to 0.3 % within 120 min (
Fig. 12 (a)). First, at higher concentrations, more dye molecules compete for adsorption onto the limited active sites of the catalyst. This has reduced the overall adsorption of dye molecules, consequently decreasing the degradation rate. Secondly, the increased concentration of dye molecules can absorb and scatter incident light, hindering its penetration into the surface of the catalyst. This reduction in light penetration has decreased the generation of the reactive species necessary for the degradation process [62]. Furthermore, additional electron-hole pairs contribute to the overall stabilization of the system and facilitate a consistent generation of radicals through the substrate-conjugated systems. This results in a slower photodegradation process and a reduction in efficiency [63]. The first order kinetic studies were evaluated using the formula (2)
(2) ln(C0/C) = Kt

where k is the reaction rate constant and t is the reaction time. Fig. 12 (b) depicts the reaction rate constant (k) of the CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposites at different dye concentrations. Fig. 12 (c) shows the first order kinetic studies for the CQDs/g-C₃N₄/TiO₂ nanocomposite of at different concentration (10, 20, 40 and 60 ppm). The rate constants for 40 and 60 ppm are very low, indicating that higher activation energy has required to initiate the reaction. Table S2 presents the first-order kinetic parameters for CQDs/g-C₃N₄/TiO₂ nanocomposites, calculated at various pH, dosages, and concentrations. The results on photocatalytic degradation of pollutants using ternary composites are presented in Table1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability of CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the long stability experiment was carried out up to 5 cycles using CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 catalyst under UV light against MB dye as shown in Fig. 13 (a). The catalyst was collected using centrifuge after each cycle by washing with water and used directly in the next cycle for degradation. The catalyst has showed very good stability with the degradation of 86.2 % of MB in the 5th cycle from 98.9 %. Also, there was no significant decrease in the degradation efficiency even after the repeated usage of the prepared catalyst. A slight decrease in the efficiency was observed because the surface of the catalyst covered with the pollutant blocks the active sites of the catalyst and also blocked the passage of light that fails to activate the catalyst [72]. The other main reason includes that during washing and centrifuge process there was a loss of the certain amount of catalyst [73]. Fig. 13 (b). shows that the there is no structural difference in the material, and it maintains the integrity and stability in both before and after reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the degradation of MB
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 14 shows the proposed photocatalytic mechanism of MB degradation by CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 nanocomposites and the schematic representation of charge carrier separation and transfer pathways of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. When a semiconductor is exposed to UV light, photons with energy exceeding the material's bandgap can excite electrons from the valence band to the conduction band, creating electron-hole pairs [74]. These results exhibit that integrating the CQDs, g-C3N4, and TiO2 nanoparticles have the narrow bandgap of the composite material. This reduced bandgap energy has necessitated a lower energy input for the electron-hole pair excitation, consequently enhancing the photocatalytic performance. The proposed mechanism shows the highly efficient photocatalytic activity in this nanocomposite heterostructures, which are facilitated by the synergistic interactions between the constituent materials. This approach holds promise for the effective removal of various organic pollutants from wastewater [75]. The experimental results have indicated that the degradation of the MB solution using the nanocomposite photocatalyst can be represented by the following Eqs. (3–9):
(3) CQDs/g-C3N4 /TiO2 + hv → CQDs/g-C3N4 /TiO2 (e−+ h+)
(4) CQDs/g-C3N4 /TiO2 (e− + h+) → g-C3N4 (e− + h+) + CQDs (e− + h+) + TiO2 (e− + h+)
(5) TiO2 (e−) + O2 → ▪O2−
(6) g-C3N4 (h+) + -OH → ▪OH
(7) ▪O2− + MB → CO2 + H2O
(8) ▪OH + O2 → ▪OOH+ ▪O2−
(9) ▪OOH + MB →CO2 + H2O

The structural parameters calculated for bulk TiO2 (space group 141/amd, a = 3.79 Å and c = 9.62Å) and g-C3N4 monolayer (space group P-6m2, a = 14.28 Å and b = 12.37Å) are in agreement with the previous reports obtained from the computational results [76]. The calculated negative formation energies of −0.63 eV for TiO2 (110)/g-C3N4 (001) and −0.58 eV for TiO2 (110)/g-C3N4 (001)/CQDs (001) heterojunctions have indicated the formation of thermodynamically stable interfaces. The values were calculated using the Eqs. (10) and (11):
(10) EFormation Energy = E TiO2 (110)/g-C3N4 (001) – (E TiO2 (110) + g-C3N4 (001))
(11) EFormation Energy = E TiO2 (110)/g-C3N4 (001)/CQDs (001) – (E TiO2 (110) + g-C3N4 (001) + CQDs (001))

where E TiO2 (110)/g-C3N4 (001) and E TiO2 (110)/g-C3N4 (001)/CQDs (001) are the total energies of the optimized heterjunctions, E TiO2 (110), g-C3N4 (001) and CQDs (001)) - are for individual systems. The Total density of states (TDOS) and projected density of states (PDOS) were calculated, as shown in
Figs. 15, 16 and S5. It can be seen in Figs. 15 and 16 that the valence band was dominated by g-C3N4 & CQDs whereas conduction band which dominated by TiO2 shows the efficient charge separation at the heterojunction. The partial density of states of TiO2 (110) (Figure S5) surface has exhibited that the valence band is mainly composed of Ti 3d whereas conduction band originates from the O 2p orbitals. In the case of monolayer g-C3N4 & CQDs, the valence band originates from C 2p and conduction band begins from N 2p orbitals (Figure S5). This shows the separation of photogenerated charge carriers.

The work function was calculated to further understand the origin of the charge transfer at the interface (Fig. 15) using the relation (12):
(12) ɸ = E vacuum – E Fermi

where ɸ, E vacuum and E Fermi represent the work function, the electrostatic potential of vacuum level and the Fermi level, respectively. The calculated work functions for heterojunctions, TiO2 (110) and g-C3N4 monolayer are 6.0, 5.52, 6.78 and 4.61 eV, respectively, are shown in Fig. 17. (a-b) and Figure S6.

In the g-C3N4 monolayer/TiO2 heterojunction, electrons tend to migrate from the g-C3N4 layer, which exhibits a lower work function towards the TiO2 (110) layer, characterized by a higher work function. The electron transfer has resulted with the accumulation of negative charges on the TiO2 surface and a corresponding positive charge on the g-C3N4 monolayer. The interfacial charge redistribution establishes an internal electric field over the heterojunction, inducing more efficient charge separation.

The calculated charge density difference (
Fig. 18 (a-b)) indicates the charge transfer from g-C3N4 & CQD monolayer to the TiO2. The DFT results have corroborated well with our experimental results for increasing the photocatalytic activity.

In conclusion, CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 ternary composite was successfully synthesized via a hydrothermal route. The morphological and structural characterization has confirmed the uniform deposition of CQDs and TiO2 nanoparticles onto the g-C3N4 sheets, leading to the formation of a well-defined composite structure. This composite exhibited a synergistic effect which has significantly improved the photocatalytic activity under UV irradiation. The degradation mechanism has involved the generation of highly reactive free radicals and superoxide radicals ·O2 −, facilitating a 98.9 % degradation rate of methylene blue (MB) within 120 min. The excellent photocatalytic performance of this composite has demonstrated its suitability for the efficient MB degradation. Therefore, this study indicates that the synthesized CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 ternary composite has the potential for various environmental applications.
